core_story.problem: Introduction Imitation learning offers an efficient solution for teach- ing robots a variety of skills by replicating behavior from experts without the need to explicitly program actions or model the environment.
core_story.limitation: Introduction Imitation learning offers an efficient solution for teach- ing robots a variety of skills by replicating behavior from experts without the need to explicitly program actions or model the environment.
core_story.insight: Our key insight is that leveraging af- fordances—manipulation priors that define “where” and “how” an agent interacts with an object—can substantially enhance generalization to entirely unseen object instances and categories.
core_story.proposed_method: We introduce the Diffusion Policy with transferable Affordance (AffordDP), designed for generaliz- able manipulation across novel categories.
core_story.experimental_evidence: Abstract Diffusion-based policies have shown impressive perfor- mance in robotic manipulation tasks while struggling with out-of-domain distributions.
core_story.summary: Introduction Imitation learning offers an efficient solution for teach- ing robots a variety of skills by replicating behavior from experts without the need to explicitly program actions or model the environment. Introduction Imitation learning offers an efficient solution for teach- ing robots a variety of skills by replicating behavior from experts without the need to explicitly program actions or model the environment. Our key insight is that leveraging af- fordances—manipulation priors that define “where” and “how” an agent interacts with an object—can substantially enhance generalization to entirely unseen object instances and categories. We introduce the Diffusion Policy with transferable Affordance (AffordDP), designed for generaliz- able manipulation across novel categories. Abstract Diffusion-based policies have shown impressive perfor- mance in robotic manipulation tasks while struggling with out-of-domain distributions.

introduction_structure.main_message: Introduction Imitation learning offers an efficient solution for teach- ing robots a variety of skills by replicating behavior from experts without the need to explicitly program actions or model the environment.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Builds context and advances the paper's argument.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Introduction Imitation learning offers an efficient solution for teach- ing robots a variety of skills by replicating behavior from experts without the need to explicitly program actions or model the environment. It has garnered significant interest across a wide range of fields, including autonomous driv- ing [13, 16, 21], robotic manipulation [3, 6, 15, 17, 22, 34, 36], and legged locomotion [20, 25, 26]. Recently, diffusion-based imitation models [6, 14, 30] have gained significant attention within the field. These models concep- tualize a robot’s visuomotor policy as a conditional diffu- sion denoising process, facilitating the learning of condi- tional action distributions.

notable_sentences: However, their generaliza- tion is typically limited to the same category with simi- lar appearances.
Abstract Diffusion-based policies have shown impressive perfor- mance in robotic manipulation tasks while struggling with out-of-domain distributions.
Addition- ally, AffordDP’s effectiveness is limited in scenarios where modeling or extracting affordances is challenging.
AffordDP mod- els affordances through 3D contact points and post-contact trajectories, capturing the essential static and dynamic in- formation for complex tasks.

By modeling affordances through 3D contact points and post-contact trajectories, and incor- porating affordance guidance during diffusion sampling, AffordDP effectively generalizes across entirely unseen ob- ject instances and categories.

Conclusion and Discussion In conclusion, the Generalizable Diffusion Policy with transferable Affordance (AffordDP) significantly advances the generalization capabilities of diffusion-based robotic manipulation policies.
Cur- rent foundational models have limitations in understanding spatial information, which can restrict AffordDP’s ability to specify affordance transfers for particular parts.
Despite these advancements, AffordDP does have some limitations that need to be addressed in future work.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version; the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.

Experimental results from both sim- ulated and real-world environments demonstrate that Af- fordDP consistently outperforms previous diffusion-based methods, successfully generalizing to unseen instances and categories where others fail.
Experimental results in both simulated and real-world environments validate the superi- ority of AffordDP over previous methods, marking a sub- stantial step forward in the robotic community.
Future work should focus on enhancing the spatial understanding of foundational models and improving affordance extrac- tion techniques to overcome these limitations.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
Diffusion-based policies have shown impressive perfor-
mance in robotic manipulation tasks while struggling with
out-of-domain distributions. Recent efforts attempted to en-
hance generalization by improving the visual feature en-
coding for diffusion policy. However, their generaliza-
tion is typically limited to the same category with simi-
lar appearances. Our key insight is that leveraging af-
fordances—manipulation priors that define “where” and
“how” an agent interacts with an object—can substantially
enhance generalization to entirely unseen object instances
and categories. We introduce the Diffusion Policy with
transferable Affordance (AffordDP), designed for generaliz-
able manipulation across novel categories. AffordDP mod-
els affordances through 3D contact points and post-contact
trajectories, capturing the essential static and dynamic in-
formation for complex tasks. The transferable affordance
from in-domain data to unseen objects is achieved by esti-
mating a 6D transformation matrix using foundational vi-
sion models and point cloud registration techniques. More
importantly, we incorporate affordance guidance during
†Equal contributions.
*Corresponding authors.
diffusion sampling that can refine action sequence gener-
ation. This guidance directs the generated action to gradu-
ally move towards the desired manipulation for unseen ob-
jects while keeping the generated action within the mani-
fold of action space. Experimental results from both sim-
ulated and real-world environments demonstrate that Af-
fordDP consistently outperforms previous diffusion-based
methods, successfully generalizing to unseen instances and
categories where others fail.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Imitation learning offers an efficient solution for teach-
ing robots a variety of skills by replicating behavior from
experts without the need to explicitly program actions or
model the environment. It has garnered significant interest
across a wide range of fields, including autonomous driv-
ing [13, 16, 21], robotic manipulation [3, 6, 15, 17, 22,
34, 36], and legged locomotion [20, 25, 26]. Recently,
diffusion-based imitation models [6, 14, 30] have gained
significant attention within the field. These models concep-
tualize a robot’s visuomotor policy as a conditional diffu-
sion denoising process, facilitating the learning of condi-
tional action distributions.
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
raw_sections.conclusion:
6. Conclusion and Discussion
In conclusion, the Generalizable Diffusion Policy with
transferable Affordance (AffordDP) significantly advances
the generalization capabilities of diffusion-based robotic
manipulation policies. By modeling affordances through
3D contact points and post-contact trajectories, and incor-
porating affordance guidance during diffusion sampling,
AffordDP effectively generalizes across entirely unseen ob-
ject instances and categories. The estimation of the 6D
transformation matrix using foundational vision models and
point cloud registration techniques allows for the transfer-
ability of these affordances. Experimental results in both
simulated and real-world environments validate the superi-
ority of AffordDP over previous methods, marking a sub-
stantial step forward in the robotic community.
Despite these advancements, AffordDP does have some
limitations that need to be addressed in future work. Cur-
rent foundational models have limitations in understanding
spatial information, which can restrict AffordDP’s ability to
specify affordance transfers for particular parts. Addition-
ally, AffordDP’s effectiveness is limited in scenarios where
modeling or extracting affordances is challenging. Future
work should focus on enhancing the spatial understanding
of foundational models and improving affordance extrac-
tion techniques to overcome these limitations.
